title: Sea-Watch Condemns EU Response After Libyan Coast Guard Attack on Rescue Vessel Sea-Watch 5
summary: Sea-Watch International strongly criticized the EU's response to the May 11 attack on the Sea-Watch 5 by Libyan coast guard vessels, calling the Commission's claim that EU-Libya cooperation 'prevented further violence' a grotesque distortion of reality.
contents:
## Sea-Watch 5 Attack and EU Response

### Overview
Sea-Watch International issued a strong condemnation on May 13, 2026, of the European Commission's response to the armed attack on rescue vessel Sea-Watch 5 by Libyan coast guard vessels on May 11. The EU stated its cooperation with Libya "prevented further violence," which Sea-Watch called "a grotesque distortion of reality."

### Attack Details
- Armed Libyan coast guard vessels opened fire on the Sea-Watch 5 on May 11 in international waters, approximately 55 nautical miles north of Tripoli
- The attack occurred shortly after the crew rescued 90 people from an overcrowded wooden boat
- Attackers fired a single shot followed by a burst of 10-15 rounds of live ammunition without warning
- Libyan militia members demanded the vessel sail to Libya and threatened to board when the crew refused
- The crew issued mayday calls and alerted Italian and German authorities

### EU Accountability
Sea-Watch spokesperson Julia Winkler stated: "The EU has worked closely with actors in Libya for years, who stand accused of committing the most serious crimes against people on the move." The attacking vessel traveled alongside the Murzuq 662 — a ship handed over by Italy to Libyan actors in June 2023 under the EU-Libya cooperation framework.

### Ongoing Operations
The Sea-Watch 5, now carrying approximately 154 rescued people after a second rescue of 64 people on May 12, headed toward the Italian port of Brindisi — more than 1,000 km from the rescue site. The ICC plans hearings on May 19-21 regarding crimes against migrants in Libya.

links.label: ABC News - Libyan vessels fire on rescue ship

title: Russia Hammers Kyiv With Massive Drone and Missile Barrage, Killing Nine
summary: Russia unleashed a third consecutive day of massive drone and missile attacks on Ukraine, demolishing an apartment building in Kyiv and killing nine people including a 12-year-old girl. Over 1,560 drones targeted Ukrainian cities since Wednesday.
contents:
## Massive Russian Aerial Assault on Kyiv

### Attack Overview

Russian forces launched 675 attack drones and 56 missiles primarily targeting Kyiv on May 14, 2026, marking the third straight day of devastating aerial bombardment. Ukrainian air defense units intercepted 652 drones and 41 missiles, achieving a 93% interception rate according to President Volodymyr Zelensky.

### Casualties and Destruction

Nine people died in the attack, including a 12-year-old girl, with dozens more sustaining injuries. A nine-story apartment building in Kyiv's Darnytskyi district partially collapsed, trapping residents under concrete rubble. Emergency workers rescued 28 people from the destroyed structure, while approximately 20 remained missing.

### Scale of Bombardment

Since Wednesday, Moscow launched more than 1,560 drones against Ukrainian population centers. The strikes damaged over 180 sites across the country, including more than 50 residential buildings, a school, and a veterinary clinic. Ukraine's air force described these attacks as among the largest Russia mounted since the full-scale invasion began in 2022.

### Diplomatic Context

The attacks coincided with US President Donald Trump's summit with Chinese leader Xi Jinping in Beijing. Ukrainian Foreign Minister Andrii Sybiha urged both leaders to pressure Moscow, stating that "only pressure on Moscow can make him stop." The EU announced finalization of a 6 billion euro drone support package for Ukraine.

### International Response

British Defence Secretary John Healey accelerated UK deliveries of air defenses. Hungary summoned the Russian ambassador over a drone attack near its border with Ukraine, marking a significant diplomatic shift under new Prime Minister Peter Magyar.

title: Vessel Seized Off UAE Coast and Taken Toward Iranian Territorial Waters
summary: UKMTO reported a vessel anchored 38 nautical miles northeast of Fujairah seized by unauthorized personnel and directed toward Iranian waters. Separately, an Indian cargo ship sank off Oman after a suspected drone or missile attack.
contents:
## Maritime Seizure Near Strait of Hormuz

### Vessel Seizure Incident

The UK Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO) issued Warning 057/26 on May 14, 2026, reporting that unauthorized personnel boarded a vessel while anchored approximately 38 nautical miles northeast of Fujairah, United Arab Emirates. The ship's CSO confirmed that the vessel now heads toward Iranian territorial waters under control of the unauthorized boarding party.

### Second Maritime Attack

In a separate incident, the Indian-flagged cargo vessel Haji Ali sank off the coast of Oman following a suspected explosion caused by a drone or missile strike. All 14 Indian crew members survived after rescue by Omani coast guard units. India's Ministry of External Affairs condemned the attack as "unacceptable."

### Floating Armoury Seized

Maritime risk firm Vanguard identified the seized vessel as the Honduras-flagged Hui Chuan, operating as a floating armoury that stores weapons for maritime security firms protecting ships from piracy. Ship-tracking data showed the vessel last broadcast its position 70 kilometers northeast of Fujairah.

### Strategic Context

These incidents occurred as senior Iranian officials reiterated claims of sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz. Iran's senior vice president stated the strait "belongs to Iran" and Tehran would not relinquish control "at any price." The seizures coincided with Trump-Xi summit discussions about keeping the strait open for commercial traffic.

title: AFP Detects 35 Chinese Vessels in West Philippine Sea During Balikatan 2026
summary: The Armed Forces of the Philippines reported 35 Chinese vessels, including PLA Navy warships and Coast Guard boats, monitored across four key locations in the West Philippine Sea from May 4-11 during Balikatan 2026 exercises.
contents:
## Chinese Naval Presence in West Philippine Sea

### Vessel Deployment Overview

The Armed Forces of the Philippines (AFP) detected 35 Chinese vessels operating across multiple features in the West Philippine Sea between May 4 and 11, 2026. AFP spokesperson for the WPS Rear Admiral Roy Vincent Trinidad confirmed the monitoring during a press briefing at Camp Aguinaldo.

### Distribution of Chinese Forces

Seven vessels (one PLA Navy ship, six Coast Guard) operated near Ayungin Shoal. Bajo de Masinloc hosted the largest concentration with 17 vessels comprising eight PLA Navy and nine Coast Guard ships. Five vessels patrolled near Escoda Shoal, while six monitored the Pag-asa Islands area.

### Balikatan 2026 Context

The vessel monitoring occurred during the recently concluded Balikatan 2026 exercises (April 20 - May 8), the annual joint military drills between the Philippines and United States. Japan, Australia, and Canada also participated. The exercises featured live-fire maritime strikes using US HIMARS, Tomahawk missiles, and Japan's Type 88 surface-to-ship missiles.

### No Aggressive Actions Recorded

Trinidad reported no coercive or aggressive actions from Chinese forces during the exercises. He noted Chinese claims of conducting combat exercises near Bajo de Masinloc, but AFP monitoring found no synchronized military activity supporting such claims. In April, 62 Chinese vessels operated in the same four areas.

### Ongoing Tensions

Beijing continues to claim almost the entire South China Sea despite a 2016 Permanent Court of Arbitration ruling that found China's expansive claims have no legal basis under international law.

title: Nearly 200 Russian Shadow Fleet Tankers Enter UK Waters Despite Boarding Threat
summary: BBC Verify analysis found 184 UK-sanctioned Russian shadow fleet vessels made 238 journeys through UK waters since PM Starmer's March threat to board them. No public evidence of any boardings has emerged.
contents:
## Russian Shadow Fleet Defies UK Enforcement

### Scale of Shadow Fleet Transits

BBC Verify analysis identified 184 UK-sanctioned vessels making 238 journeys through British waters between March 25 and May 11, 2026, despite Prime Minister Keir Starmer's announcement that armed forces could board sanctioned vessels. The vast majority comprised oil tankers (173), with 10 LNG tankers and one multipurpose vessel.

### Enforcement Gap

The Ministry of Defence claimed "disrupting and deterring" shadow fleet activity but provided no evidence of actual boardings. Former Royal Navy commander Tom Sharpe called the lack of action "utterly confusing" and "pathetic," noting the UK possesses sufficient military capability for interception.

### Legal Constraints

Shipping lawyer James Turner KC suggested legal barriers prevent seizure of vessels flying another country's flag while transiting UK waters under international law. He questioned how the policy "originated," stating it "lacks applicability unless a tanker flies a false flag or has no flag."

### Route Adaptations

Some vessels, including the Chinese-owned Yi Tong tanker, altered routes to avoid UK territorial waters by sailing around Ireland and northern Scotland. This re-routing suggests the UK's policy produces some deterrent effect, though at the cost of longer, more expensive voyages.

### EU Sanctions Expansion

The EU's 20th sanctions package, adopted April 23, added 46 shadow fleet tankers to its designation list, bringing the total to 632 vessels facing EU port access bans and maritime service prohibitions. The package also established the legal framework for a full ban on maritime services related to Russian oil.

title: SpaceX CRS-34 ISS Resupply Mission Targets May 15 Launch After Weather Scrubs
summary: NASA and SpaceX rescheduled the CRS-34 commercial resupply mission to the International Space Station for May 15 at 6:05 PM EDT after two weather-related scrubs. The Dragon spacecraft carries 6,500 pounds of science and supplies.
contents:
## SpaceX CRS-34 Mission Update

### Launch Rescheduled

NASA and SpaceX rescheduled the 34th commercial resupply services mission to the International Space Station for Friday, May 15, 2026, at 6:05 PM EDT, after poor weather forced scrubs on both May 12 and May 13. Cumulus cloud rule violations halted the May 13 countdown in its final minute.

### Cargo Manifest

The Dragon spacecraft carries approximately 6,500 pounds of supplies, including 1,834 pounds of science investigations, 1,363 pounds of crew supplies, and 1,034 pounds of vehicle hardware. Key experiments include STORIE, an instrument monitoring charged particles around Earth that affect power grids and satellites, and Green Bone, investigating bone cell growth on wood-derived scaffolds for osteoporosis treatments.

### Mission Details

A Falcon 9 rocket will launch from Space Launch Complex 40 at Cape Canaveral Space Force Station. First stage booster B1096, on its sixth flight, will attempt landing at Landing Zone 40 adjacent to the pad. Dragon spacecraft C209 also flies for a record-tying sixth mission, following qualification review of all cargo-unique hardware components.

### ISS Crew Preparation

Expedition 74 crew members Jack Hathaway and Sophie Adenot prepared Dragon cargo operations and conducted a mission readiness review with flight controllers. The crew planned to begin unpacking time-sensitive research samples from portable science freezers within hours of Dragon's arrival.

### Upcoming Station Activity

The Dragon docking follows a busy ISS schedule including an upcoming Russian segment spacewalk, Soyuz MS-29 launch in July, and the Crew-13 mission in September led by NASA astronaut Jessica Watkins.

title: Trump and Xi Agree Strait of Hormuz Must Remain Open During Beijing Summit
summary: US President Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping agreed during their Beijing summit that the Strait of Hormuz must remain open for energy flows. Iran seized a vessel off the UAE coast during the talks and demanded sovereignty recognition over the waterway.
contents:
## Trump-Xi Beijing Summit Addresses Hormuz Crisis

### Summit Outcome on Hormuz

US President Donald Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping agreed during their May 14 summit in Beijing that the Strait of Hormuz must remain open to support the free flow of energy. The White House reported Xi expressed China's opposition to militarization of the strait. The agreement came amid escalating maritime tensions in the region.

### Iranian Defiance During Summit

Iran conducted provocative actions during the summit, seizing a vessel off the UAE coast and directing it toward Iranian waters. Iranian Senior Vice President Mohammadreza Aref declared the strait "belongs to Iran" and that Tehran would not surrender control "at any price." Iran also facilitated passage of Chinese vessels through the strait under new Iranian protocols after requests from China's foreign minister.

### Iran Sets Five Preconditions for Talks

Tehran announced five preconditions for resuming negotiations: complete end to hostilities, lifting of all sanctions, release of frozen assets, war damage compensation, and formal recognition of Iranian sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz. These demands effectively preclude near-term diplomatic resolution.

### Military Intelligence Concerns

Classified US intelligence assessments, reported by The New York Times, revealed Iran retains operational access to 30 of 33 missile sites along the Strait of Hormuz and approximately 70% of its mobile missile launchers. These findings contradicted administration claims of a "decimated" Iranian military.

### Diplomatic Implications

Ukrainian Foreign Minister Sybiha urged both leaders to address Russia's aerial bombardment of Ukraine, calling the timing of massive drone strikes during the summit a "demonstration during Trump's talks in China."

title: Japan-China Military Tensions Escalate Over Taiwan Strait Transits and Southwestern Island Passages
summary: Japan-China relations deteriorated sharply after Japan's destroyer Ikazuchi transited the Taiwan Strait on April 17, prompting PLA combat readiness patrols and Chinese warship passages through Japan's southwestern islands. Analysts warn of rising clash risk.
contents:
## Japan-China Military Confrontation Intensifies

### Taiwan Strait Transit Provokes Beijing

The Japan Maritime Self-Defense Force destroyer JS Ikazuchi transited the Taiwan Strait on April 17, 2026, spending approximately 14 hours in the waterway. Beijing condemned the passage as a "deliberate provocation," noting it coincided with the 131st anniversary of the Treaty of Shimonoseki, under which China ceded Taiwan to Japan.

### PLA Response and Military Deployments

China's Eastern Theater Command deployed naval and air assets to track the Japanese vessel throughout its transit. The PLA subsequently launched combat readiness patrols in the East China Sea and dispatched warships near Okinawa. Two Chinese naval vessels sailed between Japan's southwestern islands on April 22, entering Japan's contiguous zone near Yonaguni and Iriomote islands.

### Balikatan 2026 Participation

Japan participated actively in the Philippines' Balikatan 2026 exercises for the first time, deploying Type 88 surface-to-ship missiles that successfully struck a decommissioned Philippine Navy vessel. Beijing criticized Tokyo's firing of anti-ship missiles in the Philippines, viewing the exercises as military encirclement.

### Diplomatic Deterioration

China imposed restrictions on dual-use civilian-military goods exported to Japan and tightened travel restrictions for Chinese tourists and students visiting Japan. Foreign Policy analysis warned that a limited air or naval clash between the two nations "remains plausible and not mere alarmism" given the current trajectory.

### Strategic Assessment

Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's statements about defending Taiwan, combined with accelerated deployment of long-range strike capabilities on Japanese islands, have pushed Taiwan policy to the forefront of the relationship. Beijing views these moves as preparation for military intervention in the Taiwan question.

title: Iranian Tankers Reroute Through Lombok Strait to Evade US Blockade en Route to Malaysia
summary: UANI tracking identified Iranian-flagged tankers HUGE and DERYA taking an uneconomical Indian Ocean route through Indonesia's Lombok Strait to reach Malaysia's Eastern Outer Port Limits anchorage, avoiding US naval forces.
contents:
## Iranian Shadow Fleet Adapts Routing to Evade Blockade

### New Evasion Route Detected

United Against Nuclear Iran (UANI) documented a significant new operational pattern among Iranian-flagged tankers. Vessels HUGE (IMO 9357183) and DERYA (IMO 9569700) transited Indonesia's Lombok Strait rather than the traditional Malacca and Singapore Strait routes. Both tankers briefly activated AIS signals during the Lombok Strait passage and satellite imagery confirmed their positions.

### Tanker Movements

HUGE loaded at Iran's Kharg Island on March 31, departed Chabahar on April 14, appeared near Galle, Sri Lanka on April 23, and transited through the Lombok Strait on May 3 before heading to Malaysia's Eastern Outer Port Limits (EOPL) anchorage. DERYA attempted delivery of Iranian oil at Sikka, India but departed on April 19, then also transited Lombok Strait on May 4.

### Malaysian EOPL Operations

Since February 28, UANI recorded 42 ship-to-ship transfers of Iranian oil at the EOPL anchorage approximately 70 kilometers off Malaysia's coast, all confirmed by satellite imagery. On May 11, 30 tankers listed on UANI's Ghost Armada anchored or loitered near the EOPL area with AIS signals active.

### Iranian Fleet Scale

At least 37 tankers laden with Iranian oil operate inside the Persian Gulf west of the Strait of Hormuz. Thirteen Iran-flagged tankers, laden with oil, anchored near Chabahar Port. Since the conflict's start, UANI tracked at least 70 Iranian oil loadings, representing approximately 82 million barrels generating an estimated $6 billion in revenue.

### Blockade Enforcement

US CENTCOM reported redirecting 65 commercial vessels and disabling four more since the blockade commenced on April 13. The uneconomical Lombok routing demonstrates that Iran's shadow fleet adapts rapidly to avoid US naval interdiction.

title: US Intelligence Reveals Iran Retains 30 of 33 Missile Sites Along Strait of Hormuz
summary: Classified US intelligence assessments show Iran regained operational access to 30 of 33 missile sites along the Strait of Hormuz, retaining 70% of mobile launchers and pre-war stockpile, contradicting Trump administration claims of a decimated military.
contents:
## Iran Missile Capability Assessment

### Intelligence Findings

Classified US assessments from early May 2026 revealed that Iran regained operational access to 30 of its 33 missile sites along the Strait of Hormuz, according to reporting by The New York Times. These sites can deploy mobile launchers to alternate firing locations and, in some cases, launch missiles directly from existing launchpads. Only three sites remain totally inaccessible.

### Military Stockpile Status

Iran retains approximately 70% of its mobile missile launchers across the country and roughly 70% of its pre-war missile stockpile, encompassing both ballistic missiles capable of targeting regional nations and cruise missiles for shorter-range targets. Intelligence agencies assessed that Iran regained access to approximately 90% of its underground missile storage facilities nationwide.

### Contradiction of Official Claims

The findings directly contradict months of public statements by President Trump and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth. Trump claimed on March 9 that Iran's missiles had reduced to "a scatter," while Hegseth declared on April 8 that Operation Epic Fury had "decimated Iran's military and rendered it combat-ineffective for years."

### US Munitions Depletion

The United States expended roughly 1,100 long-range stealth cruise missiles, more than 1,000 Tomahawk missiles (approximately 10 times annual procurement), and over 1,300 Patriot interceptor missiles during the conflict. Replenishing these stockpiles requires years, not months.

### Strategic Implications

Military planners avoided fully destroying some hardened underground facilities to preserve bunker-busting munitions for potential conflicts with China or North Korea. The IRGC Navy claimed an expanded "operational area" stretching 200-300 miles from Jask to beyond Qeshm Island, signaling intent to define the surrounding maritime space as under Iranian management.

title: Taiwan Detects 3 PLA Aircraft Sorties and 6 PLAN Vessels Operating Near Territory
summary: Taiwan's Ministry of National Defence reported detecting 3 PLA aircraft sorties and 6 PLAN vessels operating around Taiwan on May 14, with all three sorties entering the southwestern and eastern Air Defence Identification Zone.
contents:
## PLA Activity Around Taiwan on May 14

### Detection Report

Taiwan's Ministry of National Defence (MND) detected 3 PLA aircraft sorties and 6 PLAN vessels operating around its territory as of 6 AM local time on May 14, 2026. All three aircraft sorties entered Taiwan's southwestern and eastern Air Defence Identification Zone (ADIZ). The ROC Armed Forces monitored the situation and dispatched appropriate response assets.

### Continued Pattern

The detection followed a similar report on May 13, when the MND tracked 2 PLA aircraft sorties and 7 PLAN vessels around Taiwan, with one sortie entering the southwestern ADIZ. The sustained naval presence around the island reflects Beijing's ongoing gray zone pressure campaign.

### Annual Escalation Trend

Taiwan's defense ministry reported 3,760 PLA aircraft sorties in 2025, up from approximately 3,060 in 2024. Warship sorties reached approximately 2,640 in 2025, compared to 2,470 the previous year. The ministry warned that PLA exercises targeting Taiwan have become increasingly routine, with the risk of "turning exercises into combat" rising.

### PLA Combat Readiness Patrol

The PLA conducted a significant combat readiness patrol on May 1, deploying 28 aircraft including J-10, J-16, and KJ-500 aircraft with 15 sorties crossing the Taiwan Strait median line. These aircraft coordinated with PLAN vessels for harassment operations in Taiwan's surrounding waters.

### Trump-Xi Summit Implications

The military activity occurred during Trump's May 14-15 visit to Beijing for summit talks with Xi Jinping. Analysts noted that Taiwan's defense budget remains stalled in legislative gridlock, with competing proposals ranging from $12 billion to $40 billion, as US officials pressed for rapid passage of asymmetric defense funding.

title: US Energy Secretary Warns Iran Weeks Away from Weapons-Grade Uranium Enrichment
summary: Energy Secretary Chris Wright told the Senate Armed Services Committee that Iran stands a small number of weeks away from enriching uranium to weapons-grade level, with existing stockpiles already at 60 percent enrichment.
contents:
## Iran Nuclear Enrichment Threat Escalates

### Senate Testimony
US Energy Secretary Chris Wright delivered alarming testimony before the Senate Armed Services Committee on May 14, 2026, declaring Iran "frighteningly close" to nuclear weapons capability. Wright stated that Tehran remains only weeks away from enriching uranium to weapons-grade levels.

### Current Stockpile Assessment
Iran currently possesses uranium enriched to 60 percent, along with significant quantities enriched to 20 percent. Wright described these levels as "very concerning," noting that reaching 60 percent enrichment represents more than 90 percent of the technical journey toward weapons-grade material. The country also holds an additional 11 tons of uranium at various enrichment levels.

### Iranian Response
Ibrahim Rezaei, spokesman for Iran's parliamentary National Security and Foreign Policy Committee, threatened to raise enrichment levels to 90 percent if pressure continues, declaring "no nuclear negotiations with America." This rhetoric has intensified tensions amid the ongoing US-Iran conflict.

### Strategic Implications
Wright endorsed targeting Iran's full stockpile of enriched uranium, stating that ending Iran's nuclear program entirely represents the necessary path to global security. The testimony reinforces the Trump administration's justification for military action against Tehran's nuclear infrastructure.

title: Space Force Boosts Andromeda Space Monitoring Satellite Contract Ceiling to $6.2 Billion
summary: The US Space Force increased the contract ceiling for its Andromeda space domain awareness program from $1.8 billion to $6.2 billion, reflecting escalating orbital threat assessments projected for 2030 and beyond.
contents:
## Space Force Expands Andromeda Satellite Program

### Contract Expansion
The US Space Force announced a $4.4 billion increase to its Andromeda space-based domain awareness program, raising the contracting ceiling from $1.8 billion to $6.2 billion. The service cited a "significantly increased" fiscal 2027 Pentagon budget request driven by the "escalating threat environment projected for CY 2030+."

### RG-XX Constellation
The original Andromeda award tapped 14 companies to compete for future task orders focused on a new constellation called RG-XX, designed to replace the current Geosynchronous Space Situational Awareness Program (GSSAP) neighborhood watch satellites. The RG-XX birds will comprise smaller, lower-cost commercial satellites featuring enhanced mobility, refueling capacity, and extended operational lifespans compared to current GSSAP satellites.

### SG-XX Surveillance Birds
The expanded contract vehicle will also fund a future replacement for the classified SILENTBARKER space surveillance constellation, designated SG-XX. Space Systems Command revealed that the Space Force will operate these replacement birds independently, without partnering with the National Reconnaissance Office. The proliferated constellation will carry wide-field-of-view cameras for broad space environment surveillance.

### Launch Timeline
Budget documents indicate RG-XX launches in three increments beginning early FY29 through late FY30. SG-XX development begins with $370 million in FY27, targeting prototype launches in FY28 and operational deployment by FY30.

title: US Nuclear Weapons Production Reaches Cold War Levels Amid China Arsenal Expansion
summary: Energy Secretary Chris Wright confirmed the US now produces more nuclear weapons and plutonium pits than at any time since the Cold War, as the Senate Armed Services Committee warned of China's unprecedented nuclear buildup targeting over 1,000 warheads by 2030.
contents:
## US Nuclear Renaissance Amid Growing Threats

### Cold War-Level Production
Energy Secretary Chris Wright informed the Senate Armed Services Committee on May 14 that the National Nuclear Security Administration now delivers more new nuclear weapons and plutonium pits than at any point since the Cold War. Wright characterized this effort as part of a broader US "nuclear renaissance" under the Trump administration, with seven major warhead programs running simultaneously.

### China's Expanding Arsenal
Senate Armed Services Committee Chairman Roger Wicker warned that China has moved beyond a minimum deterrence posture, constructing hundreds of new missile silos, expanding mobile missile systems, deploying ballistic missile submarines, and investing in long-range bombers. Pentagon estimates project China's arsenal could exceed 1,000 operational nuclear warheads by 2030, up from more than 600 currently.

### Workforce Concerns
Senator Jack Reed cautioned that the NNSA already operates under significant strain, particularly after the dismissal of hundreds of trained nuclear personnel last year. Reed noted these experts remain "exceedingly hard to recruit and retain," raising questions about whether the nuclear enterprise can sustain its current operational tempo.

### Strategic Competition
Wicker framed China's nuclear expansion alongside broader military investments in shipbuilding, critical minerals, and dual-use technologies, describing the situation as "a competition we cannot afford to lose." The US currently maintains roughly 3,700 active nuclear warheads in its stockpile.

title: US Typhon Missile Launch from Philippines Draws Sharp Chinese Condemnation Amid South China Sea Tensions
summary: The United States launched a Tomahawk missile from its Philippines-based Typhon missile system, prompting China to accuse Washington of the worst provocation in years in the South China Sea and raising concerns about an arms race in the region.
contents:
## Typhon Missile Test Escalates South China Sea Tensions

### Missile Launch
The United States launched a Tomahawk cruise missile from its Philippines-based Typhon mid-range missile system, marking a significant escalation in the South China Sea military posture. Experts assessed that this move could prompt China to ramp up air defenses and deploy additional stealth strike drones throughout the contested waters.

### Chinese Response
Beijing characterized the launch as the "worst provocation" in years, reiterating demands that the Philippines withdraw US missile systems from its territory. China has repeatedly expressed alarm over the Typhon system installed in northern Luzon in 2024 and an anti-ship missile launcher deployed in 2025, calling both systems threats to regional stability.

### Philippine Position
Philippine officials, led by President Ferdinand Marcos Jr., rejected China's demand to remove the missile launchers. Philippine Ambassador Jose Manuel Romualdez confirmed discussions about deploying "upgraded" types of US missile launchers that the Philippines may eventually purchase. A US-Philippines joint statement condemned China's "illegal, coercive, aggressive and deceptive activities" in the South China Sea.

### Live-Fire Exercise Context
China's Maritime Safety Administration simultaneously announced live-fire exercises in portions of the South China Sea scheduled for May 15, restricting all vessels from entering designated areas near Shanwei. These drills represent a direct Chinese military response to the Typhon test and ongoing Balikatan exercise activities.

title: South Korea Ministry of National Defense Partners with SK Telecom to Deploy National AI Foundation Model for Defense
summary: South Korea's Ministry of National Defense signed an MOU with SK Telecom to adapt the carrier's A.X K1 AI foundation model for defense applications, marking the first military adoption of a model from the national AI foundation project.
contents:
## South Korea Advances Defense AI Sovereignty

### MOU Signing
South Korea's Ministry of National Defense and SK Telecom signed a memorandum of understanding on May 14, 2026, to apply the telecom operator's proprietary AI foundation model to the defense sector. The partnership represents the first instance of a model developed under the government's national AI foundation project entering military service.

### A.X K1 Model
SK Telecom's consortium passed the first-round evaluation with its A.X K1 foundation model, Korea's first hyperscale AI model exceeding 500 billion parameters. SK Telecom plans to provide the Ministry of National Defense with a lightweight version optimized for processing speed while reducing computational requirements.

### Resource Allocation
The Ministry of Science and ICT will supply approximately 3,000 GPUs from 10,000 government-secured units to support model development and validation. The Defense Ministry plans to begin developing the lightweight defense variant in Q2 2026, with continuous upgrades through additional training on defense-specific data.

### Strategic Significance
The collaboration carries particular weight because military AI adoption demands the highest levels of security and data sovereignty. By deploying a domestically developed model rather than relying on foreign AI systems, South Korea strengthens its defense sovereignty and reduces dependency on external technology providers. Director General Jeon Jun-beom of the Defense AI Planning Bureau pledged to "continue to expand cooperation with the private sector to effectively introduce AI across the defense sector."

title: South Korea Vows Diplomatic Action After Investigation Identifies Attacker of HMM Namu Vessel in Strait of Hormuz
summary: South Korea's Ministry of Foreign Affairs pledged appropriate diplomatic action once the party responsible for attacking the HMM cargo ship Namu in the Strait of Hormuz has been confirmed, with officials noting the attack represented the 33rd such incident in the waterway.
contents:
## South Korea Responds to Strait of Hormuz Ship Attack

### Diplomatic Pledge
A senior South Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs official declared on May 14 that Korea will take "appropriate diplomatic action" once the party responsible for the attack on the HMM cargo ship Namu gains confirmation. The official noted the Namu attack represented the 33rd such incident in the Strait of Hormuz, followed immediately by a 34th attack against a Chinese vessel.

### Investigation Progress
South Korea has transferred debris from the attack from its Dubai consulate general to the UAE embassy, initiating consultations with the UAE government to bring the evidence into the country. Once the debris arrives, a specialized investigation agency within Korea's Ministry of National Defense will conduct a thorough forensic examination.

### Attribution Challenges
Regarding the possibility that Iran carried the attack, the official acknowledged "in common sense" a third-party attacker seems unlikely but emphasized Korea "cannot tell Iran 'you must have done this' without evidence." National Security Adviser Wi Sung-lac's earlier suggestion that the weapon may have involved a missile rather than a drone remains under consideration, based on the difficulty of a drone striking the vessel's underside.

### Regional Context
The attack has intensified debate about South Korea's potential participation in the US-led freedom of navigation initiative in the Strait of Hormuz. Defense Minister Ahn Gyu-back discussed a "phased contribution" plan with US counterparts during Washington meetings this week. The incident also prompted references to a Thai vessel attack that resulted in casualties, underscoring the escalating dangers to commercial shipping in the waterway.

title: OFAC Designates 12 Entities in Latest Economic Fury Sanctions Targeting Iranian Oil Networks
summary: The U.S. Treasury Department designated 12 individuals and entities facilitating IRGC oil sales to China through shell companies in Hong Kong, Dubai, and Oman, expanding the Economic Fury maximum pressure campaign against Iran.
contents:
## OFAC Expands Economic Fury Sanctions on Iranian Oil Networks

### Designation Details

The U.S. Treasury Department's Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) designated 12 individuals and entities on May 11 for facilitating the sale and shipment of Iranian crude oil to Chinese buyers. The action targeted companies across Hong Kong, Dubai, Sharjah, and Oman that allegedly disguised the IRGC's role in petroleum exports and funneled proceeds back to Tehran.

### Targeted Entities

Among those designated, OFAC named Ahmad Mohammadi Zadeh as chief of the IRGC's Shahid Purja'fari Oil Headquarters, alongside finance chief Samad Fathi Salami. Hong Kong-based firms Hong Kong Blue Ocean Limited and Hong Kong Sanmu Limited arranged shipments aboard sanctioned tankers including the GAGAN, CANGJIE, and HASNA.

### Shadow Fleet Operations

Dubai-based Ocean Allianz Shipping LLC and Atic Energy FZE facilitated shipments on five sanctioned shadow fleet tankers throughout 2025. Oman-based Zeus Logistics Group arranged vessels to carry Iranian oil cargoes.

### Maritime Context

The designations arrive amid the ongoing U.S. naval blockade of Iranian ports, initiated on April 13. U.S. CENTCOM reported redirecting 62 commercial ships and disabling 4 since the blockade commenced. At least 37 tankers laden with Iranian oil currently operate inside the Persian Gulf, west of the Strait of Hormuz.

### Market Impact

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent warned that any person or vessel facilitating illicit oil trade risks exposure to U.S. sanctions, signaling potential secondary sanctions against foreign financial institutions and Chinese independent refineries handling Iranian crude.

title: Xi and Trump Agree on Constructive Strategic Stability Framework at Beijing Summit
summary: Chinese President Xi Jinping and U.S. President Donald Trump agreed on a new framework for 'constructive strategic stability' during their landmark summit at the Great Hall of the People in Beijing on May 14.
contents:
## Xi-Trump Beijing Summit Establishes New Bilateral Framework

### Summit Overview

Chinese President Xi Jinping hosted U.S. President Donald Trump at the Great Hall of the People in Beijing on May 14 for formal talks lasting two hours and fifteen minutes. Both leaders agreed to build a "constructive bilateral relationship of strategic stability" to guide China-U.S. relations over the next three years and beyond.

### Strategic Framework

Xi defined the constructive strategic stability concept across four dimensions: positive stability centered on cooperation, sound stability with moderate competition, constant stability with manageable differences, and enduring stability with promises of peace. He emphasized that this framework should translate into concrete bilateral actions rather than remain rhetorical.

### Key Discussion Topics

The two presidents exchanged views on the Middle East situation, the Ukraine crisis, the Korean Peninsula, and cross-strait relations. Xi described the Taiwan question as the most important issue, warning that mishandling could push both nations toward confrontation.

### Trade Outcomes

Xi referenced positive outcomes from trade negotiations between Chinese Vice-Premier He Lifeng and U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent in Seoul on May 13. Trump traveled with top executives including Nvidia's Jensen Huang, Tesla's Elon Musk, Apple's Tim Cook, and Boeing's Kelly Ortberg.

### Significance

This visit marked the first U.S. presidential trip to China in nine years. The White House readout confirmed both sides agreed the Strait of Hormuz must remain open to support the free flow of energy.

title: Japan Parliament Debates Senkaku Airspace Vulnerability After Chinese Helicopter Breach
summary: Japanese lawmakers raised alarm during a May 12 parliamentary hearing over the inability of F-15 fighters to intercept Chinese Coast Guard helicopters breaching Senkaku Islands airspace, citing the 400-kilometer distance from Naha Air Base.
contents:
## Japan Confronts Critical Senkaku Islands Defense Gap

### Parliamentary Hearing

During a May 12 parliamentary session, Japanese lawmakers pressed Defense Minister Shinjiro Koizumi on persistent airspace vulnerabilities around the Senkaku Islands. The hearing focused on a May 2025 incident when a Chinese Coast Guard helicopter entered Japanese airspace unchallenged.

### Response Time Problem

F-15 fighters scrambled from Naha Air Base, located approximately 400 kilometers from the Senkaku Islands, arrived too late to intercept the Chinese helicopter. The aircraft completed its mission and returned to its vessel before Japanese fighters reached the area, exposing a fundamental timing failure in current defense protocols.

### Persistent Chinese Presence

Chinese Coast Guard vessels now maintain near-constant positions in waters adjacent to the Senkaku Islands, operating approximately 365 days annually. These ships carry helicopters capable of rapid deployment, creating systematic opportunities for airspace violations that conventional scramble operations cannot address.

### Emerging Threat Vectors

Lawmakers identified additional concerns regarding unmanned aerial vehicles and potential robotic ground incursions on the islands. These emerging scenarios fall outside traditional military engagement rules and require entirely new defensive frameworks.

### Defense Ministry Response

Minister Koizumi acknowledged the urgency of developing new defense strategies and referenced Japan's three strategic defense documents as frameworks for implementing innovative solutions, including forward-deployed systems and enhanced surveillance networks.

title: Deadly Thunderstorms Kill at Least 89 Across Uttar Pradesh as Dust Storms Ravage Northern India
summary: Violent thunderstorms accompanied by lightning, hail, and powerful dust storms killed at least 89 people across Uttar Pradesh on May 13, with Prayagraj district reporting the highest casualties at 21 deaths.
contents:
## Devastating Thunderstorms Sweep Across Uttar Pradesh

### Casualty Report

At least 89 people perished after powerful thunderstorms swept across Uttar Pradesh on May 13, according to the state disaster management and relief office. The storms brought lightning, heavy rain, hail, and destructive winds that caused widespread damage across multiple districts.

### Worst-Hit Areas

Prayagraj district recorded the highest death toll at 21 fatalities. Sant Ravidas Nagar reported 14 deaths, Fatehpur counted 11, and Mirzapur documented 10. Additional casualties occurred across Unnao, Badaun, Bareilly, and Pratapgarh districts.

### Destruction Path

The storms uprooted trees, toppled electricity poles, and collapsed walls of homes and structures. Relief authorities confirmed 53 injuries, 87 damaged houses, and 114 livestock killed. Television footage captured billboards crashing into vehicles amid clouds of dust and debris, while roadside furniture scattered across highways.

### Infrastructure Disruption

Falling trees severed overhead electrical lines near Fatehpur, disrupting the Delhi-Howrah railway route for approximately 90 minutes. The disruption affected 22 trains passing through Kanpur. Rail service on the Prayagraj-Jaunpur and Lucknow-Pratapgarh routes also halted temporarily.

### Government Response

Chief Minister Yogi Adityanath directed district officials to complete assistance distribution within 24 hours. Prime Minister Narendra Modi expressed condolences and ordered officials to coordinate with state governments to assist affected families.

title: Xi Jinping Proposes Four-Pillar Constructive Strategic Stability Framework for China-U.S. Relations
summary: President Xi Jinping outlined a four-dimensional framework for China-U.S. constructive strategic stability during talks with Trump in Beijing, calling it 'not a slogan but concrete action.'
contents:
## Xi Defines New Architecture for China-U.S. Relations

### Framework Announcement

President Xi Jinping presented a comprehensive four-pillar definition of "constructive strategic stability" during formal talks with U.S. President Donald Trump at the Great Hall of the People on May 14. Xi stated the framework would provide strategic guidance for bilateral relations over the next three years and beyond.

### Four Stability Pillars

Xi articulated the concept through four interconnected dimensions. First, a positive stability with cooperation as the mainstay. Second, a sound stability featuring moderate competition. Third, a constant stability where differences remain manageable. Fourth, an enduring stability with achievable peace. He stressed that the framework demands concrete bilateral actions rather than remaining merely rhetorical.

### Taiwan Warning

Xi delivered pointed remarks on the Taiwan question, describing it as the most important issue in China-U.S. relations. He warned that mishandling Taiwan could lead to clashes and even conflict, pushing the entire relationship into extreme danger. He characterized "Taiwan independence" and Taiwan Strait peace as fundamentally incompatible.

### Economic Cooperation

Xi lauded trade talk outcomes from the previous day's meeting between Vice-Premier He Lifeng and Treasury Secretary Bessent in Seoul, calling the results "good news for the people of both countries." He pledged China's door to opening-up would only widen further and welcomed American companies to deepen their engagement.

### Historical Context

The summit represented the first U.S. presidential state visit to China in nine years, with Trump receiving a full military honors welcome including a 21-gun salute at Tiananmen Square.

title: Vietnam Urges U.S. to Allow Supertanker Carrying 1.99 Million Barrels Past Hormuz Blockade
summary: PetroVietnam Oil urged U.S. Naval Forces Central Command to allow the VLCC Agios Fanourios I, carrying 1.99 million barrels of Iraqi crude, to pass the U.S. blockade after a forced mid-ocean U-turn.
contents:
## Vietnam Pleads for Critical Oil Shipment Through U.S. Blockade

### Vessel Incident

The very large crude carrier Agios Fanourios I, managed by Athens-based Eastern Mediterranean Maritime, performed a mid-ocean U-turn at the approximate point where the U.S. naval blockade of Iranian ports extends. The vessel had just cleared the Strait of Hormuz carrying 1.99 million barrels of Iraq's Basrah Medium crude destined for Vietnam's Nghi Son refinery.

### Urgent Appeal

PetroVietnam Oil, the trading arm of Vietnam's state energy company, confirmed to U.S. Naval Forces Central Command that it owned the cargo and that loading occurred in Iraq, not Iran. The company described the shipment as "of extreme importance" to Nghi Son Refinery, Vietnam, and the Vietnamese people.

### Cascading Consequences

The letter warned that further delay risks halting refinery throughput with cascading consequences for millions of Vietnamese consumers, businesses, public services, and industries. Nghi Son refinery processes 200,000 barrels per day, meaning the 1.99-million-barrel cargo could sustain operations for approximately ten days.

### Blockade Context

U.S. Central Command confirmed the navy redirected the vessel to enforce the blockade against Iran, meant to prevent transit by any vessels going to or from Iranian ports. Saudi Aramco's CEO stated this week that 100 million barrels per week of supply remain locked out of markets.

### Broader Impact

The incident illustrates how the U.S. blockade now disrupts non-Iranian oil shipments transiting through the Strait of Hormuz, affecting energy-dependent nations across Asia.

title: UANI Satellite Intelligence Reveals 37 Iranian Oil-Laden Tankers in Persian Gulf Amid U.S. Blockade
summary: UANI maritime tracking identified at least 37 tankers laden with Iranian crude operating inside the Persian Gulf, while Iranian ghost fleet vessels adopt new routing through Indonesia's Lombok Strait to evade U.S. interdiction.
contents:
## Geospatial Intelligence Exposes Iranian Maritime Evasion Tactics

### Persian Gulf Concentration

The United Against Nuclear Iran (UANI) organization released its weekly maritime intelligence update on May 11, documenting at least 37 tankers laden with Iranian oil currently operating inside the Persian Gulf, west of the Strait of Hormuz. The tracking combines AIS data, satellite imagery from ESA Copernicus, and manual analyst assessment.

### Ghost Fleet Routing Shift

UANI analysts identified a significant new operational pattern among Iranian-flagged tankers. Vessels HUGE (IMO 9357183) and DERYA (IMO 9569700) transited Indonesia's Lombok Strait rather than the traditional Malacca and Singapore Strait route. This uneconomical routing through the Indian Ocean aims to avoid interdiction by U.S. naval forces positioned along conventional shipping lanes.

### Blockade Statistics

U.S. CENTCOM reported redirecting 62 commercial ships and disabling 4 vessels since the blockade commenced on April 13. Open transits through the Strait of Hormuz fell to near zero vessels per day since May 6, a dramatic decline from the pre-conflict average of 138 daily transits.

### STS Transfer Activity

Since the conflict started on February 28, UANI documented 42 ship-to-ship transfers of Iranian oil at the Eastern Outer Port Limits (EOPL) anchorage off Johor, Malaysia. On May 11, thirty tankers from UANI's Ghost Armada remained anchored near EOPL with active AIS signals.

### Revenue Estimates

UANI tracked at least 70 Iranian oil loadings since the conflict began, representing approximately 82 million barrels generating an estimated $6 billion in revenue for the IRGC.

title: Xi Jinping Warns U.S. That Taiwan Mishandling Could Trigger Military Conflict Between Both Nations
summary: During the Beijing summit with Trump, Xi Jinping delivered a stark warning that mishandling the Taiwan question could push China and the United States toward clashes and even conflict.
contents:
## Xi Delivers Pointed Taiwan Warning During Trump Summit

### Direct Statement

Chinese President Xi Jinping described the Taiwan question as the most important issue in China-U.S. relations during formal talks with U.S. President Donald Trump at the Great Hall of the People on May 14. Xi stated that proper handling would preserve overall bilateral stability, while mishandling could drive both nations toward confrontation.

### Conflict Warning

Xi warned explicitly that poor management of Taiwan-related issues could cause "clashes and even conflict, pushing the entire China-U.S. relationship into an extremely dangerous situation." He characterized Taiwan independence and Taiwan Strait peace as fundamentally incompatible concepts.

### Common Denominator

Xi framed the maintenance of peace and stability across the Taiwan Strait as the "biggest common denominator" shared by China and the United States, urging Washington to exercise "utmost caution" in handling Taiwan-related matters.

### White House Readout

The White House readout notably included that "Xi also made clear China's opposition to the militarization of the Strait and any effort to charge a toll for its use," referring to the Strait of Hormuz rather than Taiwan. Both countries agreed Iran can never have a nuclear weapon.

### Military Communication

Xi called for both sides to implement reached consensus and make better use of political, diplomatic, and military-to-military communication channels. The framework of constructive strategic stability would guide defense relations over the next three years, with expanded exchanges across multiple domains including law enforcement cooperation.

title: Philippines Records 624,400 Leaked Accounts in Q1 2026 as Data Breaches Surge 76.8 Percent
summary: Cybersecurity firm Surfshark reported a 76.8% quarter-over-quarter increase in leaked Filipino user accounts during Q1 2026, translating to approximately five accounts compromised every minute.
contents:
## Philippine Data Breach Rates Accelerate Sharply in Early 2026

### Q1 2026 Statistics

The Philippines recorded 624,400 leaked user accounts during the first quarter of 2026, representing a 76.8% increase from the previous quarter and ranking 21st globally, according to cybersecurity firm Surfshark's quarterly analysis released April 29. The figure translates to approximately five Filipino user accounts leaked every minute between January and March.

### Account Takeover Risk

Surfshark's data revealed that 51% of breached users face potential account takeover risks leading to identity theft, extortion, or other cybercrimes. The analysis placed the Philippines fourth in Southeast Asia for exposure rates.

### Cumulative Exposure

Since 2004, the country accumulated 155.6 million compromised user accounts, making it the second most affected nation in Southeast Asia. The two-decade dataset includes 57.6 million unique email addresses and 79.1 million passwords linked to Philippine accounts.

### AI-Driven Attack Surface

Breached accounts worldwide tripled in Q1 2026 compared to the same period in 2025. Surfshark identified rapid artificial intelligence adoption as a likely contributor, noting company AI usage jumped to 20.2% in 2025 from 8.7% in 2023. These AI-driven systems collect more detailed user information for automation and analytics, expanding the attack surface.

### Industry Response

Fortinet executives warned at Accelerate APAC Philippines Edition 2026 that 57% of organizations now consider AI-driven threats their top cybersecurity risk, while 54% cited fragmented tools and systems as a major vulnerability factor. Philippine enterprises reportedly juggle 20 to 40 separate security tools, creating complexity that itself amplifies risk.

title: South Korea Seeks OPCON Transfer Breakthrough Through Presidential-Level Talks
summary: Seoul pursues wartime operational control transfer from the United States through direct presidential office engagement, as military-level negotiations have stalled over timeline differences.
contents:
## South Korea OPCON Transfer Push

### Strategic Shift to Political Channels
South Korea now seeks a breakthrough in retaking wartime operational control (OPCON) from the United States through top-level political decision-making between the presidential offices of both nations. A source familiar with the matter told Yonhap News Agency that Seoul views OPCON as requiring a political and policy-level decision, best addressed at a "house-to-house" level between Cheong Wa Dae and the White House.

### Defense Minister's Washington Visit
Defense Minister Ahn Gyu-back visited Washington this week for talks with U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, partly intended to elevate the OPCON transfer issue to ministerial level. The minister acknowledged "some differences" in the U.S. position regarding transfer timelines, without elaborating on specifics.

### Divergent Timelines
Seoul assesses that it has met most requirements for the OPCON transfer and targets completion before President Lee Jae Myung's term ends in 2030, with speculation pointing to 2028. However, U.S. Forces Korea commander Gen. Xavier Brunson expressed concern about "the potential of getting driven to do something that we have not prepared for" during a military conference in Hawaii. At a Senate hearing in April, Brunson stated that "political expediency does not outpace the conditions" for the transfer.

### Security Dialogue Outcomes
The allies held their biennial Korea-U.S. Integrated Defense Dialogue (KIDD) in Washington, agreeing to deepen cooperation on "shared security goals" across the Korean Peninsula and the Indo-Pacific region. National Security Adviser Wi Sung-lac emphasized that a political decision would lead to a final agreement on the OPCON transfer, not military-to-military negotiations alone.

title: Israeli Military and Defense Firms Race to Counter FPV Drone Threats in Lebanon
summary: Israel faces an urgent operational challenge from Hezbollah's fiber-optic-guided FPV drones in Lebanon, prompting PM Netanyahu to call an emergency meeting and the defense industry to accelerate counter-drone solutions.
contents:
## Israeli FPV Drone Countermeasure Effort

### Escalating Threat
First-person-view (FPV) drones have emerged as one of the most urgent operational challenges facing Israeli Defense Forces in Lebanon. The Alma Research and Education Center reported that Hezbollah launched over 80 explosive FPV drones at IDF forces in recent weeks, killing four soldiers and one civilian while wounding dozens more. Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu reportedly called an urgent meeting on Wednesday to address the crisis.

### Technical Challenge
These drones, often guided through fiber-optic cables, prove virtually immune to electronic jamming. Smart Shooter CEO Michal Mor described them as "accessible, inexpensive, highly maneuverable, and increasingly difficult to defeat." While FPV drones transformed the Ukraine battlefield, former Israeli defense official Menahem Landau noted they represent a relatively new capability in the Middle East, where Hezbollah has rapidly adopted them.

### Industry Solutions
Multiple Israeli defense firms have stepped forward with counter-drone technologies. Smart Shooter's AI-aided fire control system, mounted on rifles, assists soldiers in targeting incoming drones. Axon Vision's EDGE ClearSky platform employs thermal sensing and AI-driven real-time processing for detecting aerial threats from armored platforms. Xtend offers interceptor drones that pursue and capture hostile FPVs using net-launching systems.

### Layered Defense Approach
Military analysts emphasize that effective defense requires a multi-layered approach combining radar, electro-optic sensors, acoustic detection, electronic warfare, and physical countermeasures. Alma founder Lt. Col. Sarit Zehavi warned that the threat could escalate from tactical to strategic if Hezbollah achieves the ability to launch swarms of dozens simultaneously, mirroring patterns observed in Ukraine.
